## [33:23 - 36:28]

And when you have successful ad sets, don't just leave them to grow old and stop working. This is what some people do. They have a successful Facebook ad set and they're like, oh, yes, this thing's working. So they might just let it run or they might duplicate it or they might height the budget and then they just leave it. And this is not what you want to do because eventually it will just stop working. And at that point, you then can't go and duplicate it because now the host is infected. You can't duplicate it once it's failed. It's just like you can't propagate dead organisms that just doesn't happen. They have to be alive to propagate and they have to be healthy too. So when they're healthy and proven over at least a four day period, start propagating them through duplication. So when ad sets are working, that's when you should duplicate them. But very important, never duplicate them until they're four days old because what might happen in that initial four day incubation period is if they look good on day one and we duplicate them, we might actually not have known that on the fourth day they were going to reveal their true nature and look unhealthy. So now what we've got is now we've got a scaled up version of some unhealthy cells and that's obviously all going to become unhealthy at scale, which is something that we don't want. So this is why we use that four day incubation period. Then we get to see the true nature of the thing. Once we know the true nature, then we're able to make a decision about scaling and propagating. And that's when we start to do that. And this is an important thing to keep in mind. And as you start testing more variations and propagating more successful blue swans, you'll have so many blue swans that you can start culling the worst performing blue swans in an effort to lift the average performance. So when you get a really good ads account set up and going, you're going to have so many blue swans that you're going to start culling the bottom quarter of your blue swans. So I know we say we don't turn off and delete blue swans, but when you start to have so many good ones, you're just starting to measure them in performance and get rid of the bottom ones and keep adding them in and keep adding them in. And each time you're really just lifting that average. And that's when you start to get a Facebook ads campaign really humming. So that's some ideas. And it's really good to really keep this in mind that you're never done with testing and you always want to keep this evergreen. You always want to be experimenting, always flushing out ads with new variations and always propagating healthy ads because eventually we know they're not going to be healthy. Now we're going to discuss the daily workflow of the king. And this is your morning routine for managing your Facebook ads.

## [36:28 - 39:28]

And this is a workflow that you should follow in a sequential process from one through to seven. So number one, complete this workflow once a day, every day at ideally 8am to 10am in the morning. And always report on yesterday's data. So you really want to do it in the morning because that's the best time to look at ads because you're going to be looking at yesterday's data. If you do it at night, well you've got to wait until after midnight to get the full day. And then what happens is it takes a while for Facebook to really aggregate all of the right numbers. So if you look just after midnight, quite often you're not seeing the full picture anyway. And you might, it's always best to wait until the morning in between about 8 and 10. And then it's good to make your changes at that time in the morning too because then it gives your ads, if you're going to propagate anything or scale it or whatever, it gives your ads that full day to experience that day. But if you do them in the middle of the day or at night, well they only really experience like half of it. And so it's good to get into the routine of doing it every day at about 8 to 10am in the morning and you always want to report on yesterday's data and you want to make your changes then and then get out. It's a once every 24 hour thing, that's it. You're not looking at them like a hawk, you know, you just come in, do your work, get out. That's it. And it shouldn't take any longer than one hour each morning. It's very simple, very simple process. And you have to be disciplined with this. ## [1:28:07 - 1:31:08]

So when the Polaris star is fine, is in alignment, we don't care about any of our other metrics. All of our other metrics can be out of KPI, provided the Polaris star is in KPI. And you always judge KPIs from their hierarchy position from the top down. So start with ROI, then cost per acquisition, cost per strategy session, cost per survey, you work your way down until you get into like CPC, CTR, all of that stuff. And you always start by judging KPIs from the top down. And vital tip number three, when observing KPIs, understand all KPIs beneath it reflect into it. And all KPIs are interconnected and none of them are isolated instances. Now this is huge, so many people don't know this. They operate Facebook as if it's a machine that's built up of isolated instances. Like their CPC is totally disconnected from their cost per acquisition and everything. And so people try and just absolutely get the cheapest CPC or the cheapest CPL. And they don't have any idea what that's doing to their profits. Now all things are connected, all of them. So whenever we're looking at KPIs, we're looking at all of them. And then only if the top ones are out, do we drill down it to the smaller ones. And if we're attempting to fix an individual KPI, we never stop observing all of the other ones. Because let's say our KPI, let's say our cost per click is $4, right? That's out of KPI. And let's say we're iterating different things to try and get it within KPI. Then let's say we bring it from $4 to $3. But in doing that, our cost per strategy session goes up from $60 to $100. How did that happen? I mean, we just made our cost per click cheaper. How on earth did our cost per strategy session get more expensive? That's what I mean. We've got to watch out for that stuff.
